title: Fire-and-forget TRANSFER with no atomicity or rollback
description: The TRANSFER command debits the sender's account (line 70: counter -= amount) before sending the CREDIT to the receiver (line 74: send_udp(host, peer_port, credit_msg)). The send_udp() function returns -1 on failure (line 24) but the return value is never checked. An OK reply is sent to the client regardless of whether the credit was successfully delivered (line 77). If the UDP packet is lost or the receiver is unreachable, funds are lost permanently with no rollback mechanism.
why_bug: UDP is an unreliable transport with no delivery guarantee. The debit is applied immediately, but the credit send is fire-and-forget. Network failure between DEBIT and CREDIT delivery causes money loss. The protocol lacks atomic two-phase commit semantics required for financial operations.
suggested_fix: Implement a reliable transfer protocol: (1) use a sequence number and per-txid dedup state on the receiver; (2) require ACK from receiver before sending OK to sender; (3) implement automatic retry with timeout; (4) if credit delivery fails, rollback the debit or queue for later retry. Or add a separate reconciliation/audit log to detect and correct dropped credits.
verify_justification: The vulnerability is confirmed. In counter.c lines 196-204, the TRANSFER command debits the sender's counter immediately (line 196: counter -= amount) before sending a fire-and-forget CREDIT message (line 202: send_udp(host, peer_port, credit) with return value unchecked). An OK reply is unconditionally sent to the client (line 204) regardless of whether send_udp succeeded or failed. Since UDP is unreliable and sendto() can fail silently, network failure between DEBIT and CREDIT delivery causes permanent fund loss with no rollback mechanism. The return value of send_udp() is discarded, so the sender never learns if the credit was actually delivered.
status_correction: The file path should be counter.c (not counter.py as stated in the claim), but the severity, confidence, and core technical issue are accurately characterized. The status_vs_c_version value of "carried-over" cannot be verified without access to a C version for comparison, but the fire-and-forget pattern described matches the actual implementation.

title: Constant hardcoded txid 'tx123' enables credit duplication attacks
description: The transfer() function always constructs TRANSFER messages with txid='tx123' (line 99: msg = f"TRANSFER tx123 {to_host} {to_port} {amount}"). The node's CREDIT handler at line 58 applies counter += amount without checking whether this txid has already been credited. On UDP retransmit or replay, the same CREDIT is processed multiple times, incrementing the counter for each duplicate.
why_bug: All transfers use the same txid, so the receiving node cannot distinguish between the first CREDIT and a retransmit. With no idempotency enforcement (e.g., a set of seen-txids), the receiver blindly applies every CREDIT, doubling or tripling the effect on retry.
suggested_fix: Generate unique transaction IDs (e.g., UUID or monotonic counter). Maintain a dedup set on each node: track seen txids per peer. Before applying a CREDIT, check if txid was already seen; if yes, send OK but don't modify the counter. Periodically purge old txids from the dedup set.
verify_justification: The hardcoded txid vulnerability is confirmed in counter.c at line 251 where the transfer() function constructs "TRANSFER tx123 %s %s %d", with explicit comments (lines 249-250) stating "ADDED PLACEHOLDER 'tx123' AS THE FIRST PARAMETER AFTER TRANSFER // fix later". The CREDIT handler (lines 184-191) unconditionally increments counter without any deduplication logic: counter += amount followed by send_reply, with txid only used for logging. UDP's unreliability means retransmitted CREDIT messages will be processed multiple times, each incrementing the counter, enabling credit duplication attacks.
status_correction: The status_vs_c_version claim of "carried-over" is questionable—the code contains an explicit placeholder comment suggesting intentional stub code rather than a bug carried from a prior C version. However, the vulnerability itself is present and unmitigated in the current code.

title: No validation of transfer amount: negative amounts mint funds, positive amounts allow overdraft
description: TRANSFER command at line 68 parses amount = int(parts[4]) with no validation. Negative amounts cause counter -= (negative) which adds funds (line 70). Positive amounts cause counter -= (positive) which subtracts, but there is no check that counter >= amount, allowing the balance to go negative.
why_bug: No input validation. A TRANSFER of -100 units increases your balance. A TRANSFER of 1000 units from an account with 10 units results in balance -990. Both violate basic accounting invariants. The same holds for CREDIT (line 57) which directly adds the unvalidated amount.
suggested_fix: Validate all amounts: (1) amount > 0 (reject zero and negative); (2) for TRANSFER/DEBIT, check counter >= amount before subtracting, reject if insufficient; (3) for CREDIT, validate amount > 0 before adding. Return error reply if validation fails.
verify_justification: The claim accurately identifies critical validation vulnerabilities in counter.c (not counter.py as stated). Line 186 executes `counter += amount;` for CREDIT with zero validation, allowing negative amounts to decrement balance and missing source verification. Line 196 executes `counter -= amount;` for TRANSFER with zero validation, allowing negative amounts to add funds and positive amounts to overdraft (no check that counter >= amount before subtraction). Both commands parse integers via sscanf without subsequent validation, directly violating accounting invariants. The vulnerabilities exist exactly as described, though the file path references Python when the actual implementation is C.
status_correction: The status should be "carried-over" is accurate regarding the vulnerability itself. However, note: the claim specifies counter.py (which doesn't exist) when the actual vulnerable code is counter.c (C implementation). If "carried-over" means this vulnerability exists in the current version, that is confirmed. If it implies equivalence to a prior Python version, that cannot be verified without seeing the prior version.

title: No authentication or source verification on RESET/TRANSFER/CREDIT/STATE commands
description: All commands (STATE, CREDIT, TRANSFER, RESET) are accepted from any UDP source without verification. The addr variable is captured from recvfrom() (line 46) but never checked or validated. An attacker on the network can send forged RESET commands to zero any node's balance, or inject fake CREDIT messages to mint funds.
why_bug: UDP has no built-in authentication; an attacker can forge the source IP. Without validating the peer's identity or having a list of authorized peers, any UDP packet is processed as legitimate.
suggested_fix: Implement authentication: (1) configure a static list of trusted peer IPs/ports; (2) validate the source addr against this list before processing; (3) alternatively, use HMAC-SHA256 or similar to sign every message with a shared secret, verify the signature on receipt; (4) or run the counter nodes inside a secure network boundary (e.g., isolated VLAN or firewall rules limiting UDP to trusted IPs).
verify_justification: The counter.c node function (lines 179-212) processes all STATE, CREDIT, TRANSFER, and RESET commands from any UDP source without authentication. The peer address is captured via recvfrom() at line 174 but never validated against any whitelist or authorization mechanism. Line 186 allows CREDIT to directly increment the counter, line 196 allows TRANSFER to decrement it, and line 208 allows RESET to arbitrarily set the counter value—all without source verification. No HMAC, signature validation, or authorized peer list exists anywhere in the codebase. An attacker on the network can forge UDP packets to manipulate any node's balance. Note: The claim incorrectly references counter.py (should be counter.c) and lines 44-89 (should be 179-212 where commands are actually processed).
status_correction: Confirmed as a critical vulnerability in the actual code. The status_vs_c_version claim of "carried-over" cannot be independently verified without the C version baseline, but the current implementation definitively has no authentication or source validation.

title: Broad 'except Exception' silently swallows all errors, masks bugs and network issues
description: The entire node() loop (lines 44-89) is wrapped in a try-except that catches Exception and does nothing (pass). This silently swallows: (1) recvfrom() network errors; (2) decode('utf-8') errors on malformed input; (3) int() parsing errors on invalid amounts/ports; (4) split() parsing errors; (5) socket.sendto() errors when replies fail to send. All errors are silent; there is no logging, no error count, no indication that anything went wrong.
why_bug: Exception handling in the C version is explicit (if n < 0 continue; for recvfrom errors). Python's broad except is much more dangerous because it hides programming bugs (e.g., NameError, KeyError, AttributeError) alongside legitimate network errors. A developer adding new code might introduce a typo that silently fails. Network issues (persistent recvfrom() failures) produce no log entry, making debugging impossible.
suggested_fix: Replace broad except Exception with specific exception types: (1) except socket.error: handle network errors only; (2) add logging.error() or print() to stderr when errors occur; (3) move int() conversions to separate try-except blocks with error replies; (4) never catch Exception—only catch the specific exceptions you expect. Consider adding a flag to count/log errors for monitoring.
verify_justification: The claim is confirmed. Lines 44-89 of counter.py on the bug-hunt-claude-screen branch contain a while True loop wrapped in try-except that catches all Exception types with a bare pass statement (line 88-89). This broad exception handling silently swallows: (1) socket.recvfrom() errors (line 46), (2) data.decode('utf-8') errors (line 47), (3) int() conversion errors (lines 57, 68, 81), (4) socket.sendto() errors (lines 51, 61, 77, 85, 87), and any programming bugs. The C version (counter.c lines 174-176) explicitly checks if recvfrom returns n < 0 and continues, providing specific error handling without catching unintended exceptions. The status_vs_c_version is correctly identified as "new" since this broad exception pattern only exists in the Python version.
status_correction: confirmed

title: Malformed/truncated commands receive no reply, breaking request-reply symmetry
description: Commands like CREDIT and TRANSFER require a minimum number of parts (lines 54, 64). If a client sends 'CREDIT tx1 nodeA' (3 parts instead of 4 required), the len(parts) check fails (line 54: if len(parts) >= 4:), and the if-block is skipped. The code does NOT send an error reply; instead it falls through the elif chain and continues to the next iteration of the loop, leaving the client hanging without response. Only completely unrecognized commands (those that don't match any startswith) reach the else clause (line 86) and get an ERR reply.
why_bug: The code structure uses elif chains, so if an if-block is skipped due to a length check, the entire command is dropped with no reply. The C version uses sscanf with a return-value check: if the pattern doesn't match (e.g., wrong number of fields), the else-if fails and execution falls through to the else clause (line 213-215 in counter.c), which sends an ERR. Python doesn't have this fallback.
suggested_fix: After parsing each command, if validation fails, send an error reply before continuing. Restructure to: if startswith('CREDIT') and len parts == 4: process; elif startswith('CREDIT'): reply 'ERR CREDIT'; similar for TRANSFER, RESET. Or use a single try-parse wrapper that sends ERR on any parse failure.
verify_justification:
The claim is CONFIRMED. Analysis of src/counter.py (lines 52-87, available on bug-hunt-claude-screen branch) shows a genuine bug: when a CREDIT command with insufficient parts (e.g., 3 parts instead of 4) is received, the condition at line 52 `elif buf.startswith("CREDIT ")` is True, but the inner validation at line 54 `if len(parts) >= 4:` is False. This causes the nested if-block to be skipped, and because the elif condition itself was True, execution does NOT fall through to the else clause at line 86-87. Instead, the loop continues without sending any reply, breaking request-reply symmetry. The C version (lines 184-216) uses sscanf with return-value checks, guaranteeing that failed parses reach the final else clause for an ERR reply. This is a legitimate bug introduced in the Python rewrite.</anification>
<parameter name="status_correction">confirmed as "new" - The bug did not exist in the C version due to its sscanf-based validation approach</an_correction>
</invoke>

title: request_udp() accepts replies from any source, no source-address validation
description: The request_udp() function sends a UDP packet to a target host:port, then calls sock.recvfrom(BUF) at line 32 to receive the reply. The source address is returned in the addr variable but never checked. Any UDP packet arriving at the socket is accepted as the reply, regardless of whether it came from the intended destination.
why_bug: An attacker on the network can send a forged UDP packet with spoofed source IP to intercept replies. For example, when a client calls request_udp(..., '127.0.0.1', 5000, ...), an attacker can send a packet that looks like it came from 127.0.0.1:5000 and inject a false response (e.g., 'OK TRANSFER' when the actual transfer failed).
suggested_fix: After recvfrom() at line 32, extract addr and compare it to (host, port). If addr doesn't match the intended destination, discard the packet and wait for the next one (or timeout). In Python: if addr[0] != host or addr[1] != int(port): continue_wait.
verify_justification: The vulnerability is confirmed in the C code (not Python). The request_udp() function at lines 88-130 in /Users/nparekh/source/223b-distributed-systems/project/dead-nodes-tell-no-tales/src/counter.c exhibits the exact security flaw described. At line 120, the code calls recvfrom(sock, reply, BUF - 1, 0, NULL, NULL) with NULL passed for both the source address and address length parameters, meaning it completely ignores the source address of incoming packets. The function sends a UDP packet to a specific host:port (resolved at line 98), but then accepts any UDP reply on that socket without validating it came from the intended destination (line 120). An attacker on the network could send a spoofed UDP packet with a forged source IP and inject a false response, and the function would accept it as the legitimate reply. The status_vs_c_version should be "fixed" rather than "carried-over" since this is C code, not Python; however, the vulnerability itself in the C implementation is definitely present and unpatched.
status_correction: The file is counter.c, not counter.py. The status_vs_c_version label "carried-over" is misleading—this IS the C version and the vulnerability exists in the C code itself. If there was a Python version, it may have had the same bug that was "carried over" to C, but the current claim file path is incorrect (counter.py does not exist).

title: recvfrom(512) truncation can silently drop or corrupt long messages
description: The socket.recvfrom(BUF) call at line 46 reads up to 512 bytes. If a UDP packet larger than 512 bytes is received, it is silently truncated to 512 bytes. The decode('utf-8') at line 47 will decode the truncated buffer, potentially losing command data or producing a malformed message that causes a silent drop or incorrect parse.
why_bug: BUF=512 is a fixed limit. UDP packets can be up to 65535 bytes. If a TRANSFER message with a long destination hostname or an unusually large amount is sent in a >512 byte packet, the message is corrupted. The code has no check for datagram truncation (socket.MSG_TRUNC flag).
suggested_fix: Use socket.recvfrom(BUF, socket.MSG_TRUNC) to detect if the packet was truncated (the returned length will be > BUF). If truncation is detected, send an error reply and discard the message. Alternatively, increase BUF to a larger value (e.g., 8192 or 65535) or add length validation to reject packets that might be malformed.
verify_justification: The recvfrom(512) truncation vulnerability is confirmed in counter.c at line 174 (the claim cited line 46, which is incorrect—that line contains only a function declaration). The node() function receives UDP messages with `recvfrom(sock, buf, sizeof(buf) - 1, 0, ...)` where buf is char buf[BUF] (BUF=512, line 11). This reads at most 511 bytes without detecting truncation via MSG_TRUNC flag. If a UDP packet exceeds 512 bytes, it silently truncates, and the message parsing via sscanf() on line 192 can silently fail (returning fewer than 4 fields for TRANSFER messages), causing malformed messages to be treated as errors instead of detected as truncation—losing command data without alerting the sender. The vulnerability does exist, but the file is counter.c (not .py) and the primary vulnerable line is 174 (not 46). Status "carried-over" is accurate as this is C code exhibiting the claimed defect.
status_correction: Status is correct (carried-over), but file extension and line numbers are wrong: should be counter.c line 174 (and secondarily line 120), not counter.py line 46.

title: int() conversion raises ValueError, hidden by broad except Exception
description: Python's int(string) raises ValueError if the string is not a valid integer. The code calls int() on user input at lines 40 (port), 57 (amount in CREDIT), 68 (amount in TRANSFER), 81 (amount in RESET), and 130-132 (ports/expected/timeout in sum_counters). Any of these can raise ValueError. However, the broad except Exception at line 88 catches and silently swallows these errors, so invalid input produces no error reply, just a silent drop.
why_bug: The C version uses strtol() which silently converts to 0 on invalid input (and returns the converted value). Python's int() raises an exception. Both are broken, but in different ways: C ignores bad input silently; Python also ignores it (via the broad except). However, Python's behavior is less predictable because int('abc', 5000, 10) raises immediately, while in C the error is implicit.
suggested_fix: Wrap int() conversions in specific try-except blocks that catch ValueError and reply with an error (e.g., 'ERR INVALID_AMOUNT'). Do NOT rely on the broad except Exception. Or move int() conversion before the main node loop to catch startup errors early.
verify_justification: The claim correctly identifies that Python's int() raises ValueError on invalid input (lines 40, 57, 68, 81, 130-132 of the historical counter.py from commit 04a12da). Lines 57, 68, 81 ARE caught and silently swallowed by the broad except Exception at line 88 in the node function's while loop (lines 45-88). However, line 40 (counter = int(init_counter)) is outside this try block and can raise ValueError at startup. Lines 130-132 in sum_counters are also unprotected. The comparison to C's strtol() is accurate - strtol silently returns 0 on bad input, while Python's int() raises ValueError. The status_vs_c_version being "changed" is correct: both are broken, but in different ways. The broad except at line 88 does hide the exceptions silently without sending error replies to clients (as evidenced by the bare pass statement).
status_correction: The claim's status_vs_c_version is correctly marked "changed". The C implementation (strtol with silent fallback to 0) and Python implementation (int with uncaught ValueError at startup/sum_counters, caught but silent in node function) handle invalid input fundamentally differently.

title: Execution depends on relative path ./counter.py, fails if CWD is wrong
description: Tests call ./counter.py (e.g., test/test_local_b.sh line 12) expecting the script to be executable via relative path from the test/ directory. The shebang at line 1 (#!/usr/bin/env python3) works, but requires CWD to be set correctly. If tests are run from a different directory or counter.py is moved, the relative path breaks.
why_bug: Shell scripts use relative paths (./counter.py) instead of absolute paths. In CRIU checkpoint/restore scenarios or container orchestration, the CWD may not be what the test assumes, causing 'command not found' errors.
suggested_fix: Use absolute paths in tests: /work/src/counter.py (as in repro_counter_py.sh). Or set up PATH or use $(dirname $0)/../src/counter.py to derive the absolute path. In the main() function, consider accepting an optional --config flag to load JSON/YAML configuration with explicit paths.
verify_justification: The claim about relative path dependency is PARTIALLY ACCURATE but overstated. At commit 5845784 (when counter.py was introduced), test/test_local_b.sh does invoke ./counter.py (line 12+), and counter.py has #!/usr/bin/env python3 as its shebang (line 1). However, the risk is MITIGATED by the container setup. In Containerfile.test, WORKDIR is set to /test, src/counter.py is COPYed directly to the root of that directory (with COPY src/counter.py .), and the file is made executable (RUN chmod +x counter.py). When run_tests.sh executes, it's in /test directory where counter.py exists, so ./counter.py works as intended. The claim correctly identifies that IF the CWD changes or counter.py is moved to a subdirectory (e.g., /test/src/), the relative path would break. However, the current container setup makes this fragile rather than broken—it "works as wired but fragile" (as stated in finding 09-merge-impact.md, line 111). The claim's severity and suggested fixes are reasonable, but the claim's statement that tests call counter.py expecting "the script to be executable via relative path from the test/ directory" is technically correct only because the container copies it there. The risk is real but contingent on deployment practices, not an immediate defect in the current setup. Status_vs_c_version "carried-over" is INCORRECT—this is a NEW issue introduced with counter.py; counter.c had no relative path invocation issue. The prior version (commit c9c9280) used /usr/local/bin/counter (absolute path), not ./counter.
status_correction: new (not carried-over) — introduced when switching from counter.c (invoked via /usr/local/bin/counter with absolute path) to counter.py (invoked via ./counter.py with relative path) in commit 5845784.

title: No snapshot export path reached (no __START_SNAPSHOT__ marker emitted)
description: The counter.py code has no snapshot-related logic. There is no __START_SNAPSHOT__ marker, no print statement to signal snapshot readiness, and no code path that suspends or prepares for CRIU checkpoint. In contrast, the proxy/ directory likely contains snapshot_handler.py which expects to see __START_SNAPSHOT__ on stdout. Without this marker, the CRIU harness may timeout waiting for the app to be ready for checkpointing.
why_bug: The app needs to signal when it has completed initialization and is ready for snapshot. The absence of this signal breaks the checkpoint-restore workflow. The C version also has no snapshot code, so this is a carried-over architectural issue (not a Python-specific bug).
suggested_fix: Add a snapshot-ready signal: after binding the socket and entering the listen loop, print '__START_SNAPSHOT__' to stdout (or emit a specific log line the harness watches for). See proxy/snapshot_handler.py to understand what signal is expected. Alternatively, implement a status endpoint (e.g., HTTP or UDP /status) that signals readiness.
verify_justification:
The claim is confirmed. The counter.py file (209 lines) retrieved from git commit 04a12da contains no snapshot-related code. Specifically: (1) there is no `__START_SNAPSHOT__` marker emitted anywhere in the file (verified via full text scan), (2) the only initialization signal is line 44 where the node prints "node {name} listening on udp/{port} with counter={counter}" but does NOT include any snapshot-ready signal, (3) there are no CRIU checkpoint preparation mechanisms (no imports, no signal handlers, no state suspension logic). The snapshot_handler.py in proxy/ (line 236 of mesh_proxy.py) expects to receive data starting with `b"__START_SNAPSHOT__"` from the local application via the transparent proxy, but the counter application never sends this. This is documented as a carried-over architectural issue in 09-merge-impact.md line 59: "C9 app never emits `__START_SNAPSHOT__` | same | (none) | **carried-over** (snapshot path still dead)". The status_vs_c_version assessment is accurate: this is "carried-over" because the original counter.c also lacks this signal (per findings document C9, lines 115-123).</justification>
<parameter name="status_correction">confirmed - status_vs_c_version is correctly labeled "carried-over"

title: MESH_SUBNET unbound variable in run_test_suite.sh
description: run_test_suite.sh uses 'set -euo pipefail' (line 3) which enforces all variables to be bound before use. At line 35, it passes '-e MESH_SUBNET="$MESH_SUBNET"' to the sidecar-test container, but MESH_SUBNET is never defined in the script. With 'set -u', this will cause immediate failure with 'unbound variable' error. Line 26 names the test container 'test-container' but line 33 tries to attach to it after it exits and has been removed (--rm flag), making the --network reference invalid.
why_bug: MESH_SUBNET should be computed like in build.sh (line 26: MESH_SUBNET="10.24.24.0/24"), or read from an environment variable, or omitted if auto-detection is preferred (proxy/entrypoint.sh supports this). The 'set -u' strict mode combined with undefined MESH_SUBNET causes shell to exit with error before any containers run.
suggested_fix: Define MESH_SUBNET before use: add 'MESH_SUBNET="10.24.24.0/24"' after line 3 in run_test_suite.sh. Also, the sidecar attachment at line 30-36 cannot succeed because test-container no longer exists (removed by --rm at line 19)—either remove --rm, or redesign to attach sidecar before test-container exits.
verify_justification: The claim references run_test_suite.sh line 35 as having an unbound variable issue with MESH_SUBNET in active code. However, the entire sidecar attachment block (lines 29-35) is commented out in run_test_suite.sh. All these lines begin with '#', making them shell comments that are never executed. Therefore, the unbound variable error would never occur in run_test_suite.sh because the code never runs. The issue IS real in run_test_proxycontainer.sh (where lines 30-36 are active code that reference $MESH_SUBNET without defining it and would fail under 'set -u'), but that's a different file. Additionally, the claim's container lifecycle concern is also moot in run_test_suite.sh since the sidecar code is commented; however, in run_test_proxycontainer.sh, the container named "test-container" created with --rm at line 19-27 would indeed be removed before the sidecar tries to attach at line 33, making that reference invalid.
status_correction: false_positive for run_test_suite.sh (code is commented); real issue in run_test_proxycontainer.sh (active code with unbound MESH_SUBNET at line 35 and invalid container attachment at line 33 due to --rm flag)

title: sidecar image never built in run_test_suite.sh
description: build.sh (lines 12-13) builds both 'counter' and 'sidecar' images. run_test_suite.sh (line 7) builds only 'test-runner' via Containerfile.test, but then attempts to run a 'sidecar' container (line 32) that was never built. This will cause 'podman run' to fail with 'image not found: sidecar'.
why_bug: Image renames reorganized build outputs to 'counter', 'sidecar', 'test-runner' but run_test_suite.sh forgot to add the sidecar build step. The Containerfile.rudp exists and is correct, but is not invoked by run_test_suite.sh.
suggested_fix: Add line after line 7 in run_test_suite.sh: 'sudo podman build --network=host -t sidecar -f Containerfile.rudp .'
verify_justification: The claim is substantially accurate with minor technical details. Examination of run_test_suite.sh (lines 1-35) confirms: (1) line 7 builds only 'udp-test-runner' via Containerfile.tests; (2) lines 29-35 contain commented-out code that references 'rudp-sidecar' image; (3) the sidecar image is never built by run_test_suite.sh—it exists only in proxy/build.sh line 5 as 'rudp-sidecar'. However, the actual test_local_b.sh (run inside the container) expects pre-existing containers named 'counter-b' and 'sidecar-b' (lines 26-27, 38), yet run_test_suite.sh neither builds the sidecar image nor creates these containers. The merge impact assessment (claude_screen/findings/09-merge-impact.md) confirms this issue was "relocated" from the deleted run_test_proxycontainer.sh into run_test_suite.sh, noting the new image name should be 'sidecar' (not 'rudp-sidecar'). The claim slightly misstates the line number (lines 29-35 not 32 are commented-out sidecar reference) and image name ('rudp-sidecar' in code, claimed as 'sidecar'), but correctly identifies that the sidecar image is never built by run_test_suite.sh, creating a runtime failure when tests execute.
status_correction: new (confirmed)—this is a novel bug introduced during the recent reorganization, as documented in the merge impact assessment

title: test-container already removed when sidecar-test attaches to it
description: Line 19 runs test-container with '--rm -it' (foreground, auto-remove on exit). The script blocks at line 19 until the container exits. Once it exits, --rm immediately deletes it. Then lines 30-36 attempt to attach sidecar-test with '--network container:test-container', but test-container no longer exists, causing failure.
why_bug: The test harness was reorganized to separate test execution from sidecar setup, but the network attachment ordering breaks. The '--rm' flag paired with '-it' (interactive foreground) makes the subsequent attachment impossible.
suggested_fix: Remove '--rm' from line 19 so test-container persists after the test completes. Or, redesign to attach sidecar before test-container exits. The sidecar attachment can happen in a second foreground run after test-container finishes, but only if --rm is removed.
verify_justification:
The bug logic is technically valid and the vulnerable code pattern EXISTS in run_test_proxycontainer.sh (lines 19-36), but the claim contains a CRITICAL FILE REFERENCE ERROR. The claim specifies "run_test_suite.sh" as the target file, but the actual vulnerable code is in run_test_proxycontainer.sh. In run_test_suite.sh, line 19 runs `sudo podman run --rm -it ... udp-test-runner` WITHOUT an explicit `--name test-container` flag, and lines 30-36 contain commented-out code referencing `container:udp-test-runner`, not `test-container`. The run_test_proxycontainer.sh file DOES contain the exact pattern described: line 19-27 runs with `--rm -it --name test-container`, and lines 30-36 attempt to attach with `--network container:test-container`. The bug mechanism is accurate: `--rm -it` runs the container in foreground, blocks script execution, and upon exit immediately deletes it, making subsequent network attachment impossible. However, the claim's file path and line number mappings do not match run_test_suite.sh.</anification>
<parameter name="status_correction">The status_vs_c_version claim of "new" is correct. Commit 3eac4ef (Mon Jun 8 16:19:20 2026) first introduced run_test_proxycontainer.sh with this code pattern. The code has not been present in prior versions and exists only in recent commits on claude-bug-hunt, bug-hunt-claude-screen, and main branches.</an_correction>
</invoke>

title: Unquoted variable in podman logs command
description: build.sh line 47 runs 'sudo podman logs -f $SIDECAR_NAME' without quoting the variable. While build.sh does not use 'set -u', bash still requires quoting for proper word splitting and safety. If $SIDECAR_NAME contains spaces (unlikely but possible), the command may misbehave. More importantly, using unquoted variables is a bash best practice violation.
why_bug: Variables should be quoted to prevent word splitting and globbing, even when not strictly required by 'set -u'. This is a code quality issue that makes the script fragile.
suggested_fix: Change line 47 to: 'sudo podman logs -f "$SIDECAR_NAME"'
verify_justification: The claim is technically accurate but contains a line number error. Line 49 in build.sh (not 47) contains the unquoted variable: 'sudo podman logs -f $SIDECAR_NAME'. The script does include 'set -u' on line 3, making unquoted variables a violation of the strict mode's intent. The variable $SIDECAR_NAME is defined on line 17, so the script currently works, but quoting it ('sudo podman logs -f "$SIDECAR_NAME"') is the correct bash best practice to prevent word splitting. This is confirmed as a "new" issue—it was first introduced in commit 9ff8a61 (Prince Modi, June 9 13:42:59 UTC, "L").
status_correction: new - confirmed as introduced in commit 9ff8a61

title: Aggressive cleanup with 'sudo podman rm -fa' nukes all host containers
description: build.sh line 11 runs 'sudo podman rm -fa' which removes ALL containers on the entire Podman host (system-wide, not scoped to this project). This is destructive and can impact other work running on the same machine. The script also assumes a 'vlan' macvlan network exists (line 34) without creating or validating it.
why_bug: The '-a' flag removes all containers, not just ones created by this script. The '-f' flag forces removal. Together they are too broad. This makes the script unsafe to run in shared environments.
suggested_fix: Remove the line entirely, or replace with selective cleanup: 'sudo podman rm -f counter-$SUFFIX sidecar-$SUFFIX 2>/dev/null || true' (only removes containers this script would create). Verify the 'vlan' network exists before using it, or create it if missing.
verify_justification: Line 11 of /Users/nparekh/source/223b-distributed-systems/project/dead-nodes-tell-no-tales/build.sh contains 'sudo podman rm -fa', which removes ALL containers system-wide using the '-a' (all) and '-f' (force) flags per podman documentation. This is destructive to any other containers on the host. The script later uses '--replace' (lines 34, 42) which would recreate containers anyway, making this aggressive cleanup particularly problematic. Commented cleanup code at lines 29-30 shows a safer pattern ('sudo podman rm -f "$APP_NAME" "$SIDECAR_NAME"') that only targets this script's containers, indicating the line 11 behavior is likely unintended.
status_correction: The status_vs_c_version as "new" cannot be independently verified without version history, but the vulnerability is present in the current code.

title: test_local_b.sh removes counter-b and sidecar-b before A->B transfer while dead-node test is active
description: The test checkpoints counter-b and sidecar-b (lines 26-27), removes them (line 29), then sends a transfer from A to B (line 33) while both are dead. The sidecar-a will probe sidecar-b, timeout after 0.1s (mesh_proxy.py line 199), mark it EXTERNAL (line 202), and send the transfer packet directly to B's IP without RUDP reliability. Since counter-b doesn't exist, the packet is lost. When counter-b is restored (line 37), the transfer is not retransmitted because RUDP credits are not re-attempted for EXTERNAL routes (mesh_proxy.py lines 301-303 just send directly). The test expects the final sum to be 30 but the transferred 7 credits never reach B.
why_bug: The sidecar proxy's fallback strategy for dead peers is to mark them EXTERNAL and send unreliably without queuing/retransmission. The test's setup removes the peer before sending, which triggers this fallback, dropping the packet. The comment on line 31-32 says 'Sidecar A will intercept this, queue it, and persistently retry' but the actual code (mesh_proxy.py) doesn't persist retries for EXTERNAL peers—it only buffers during the PROBING phase.
suggested_fix: Either: (1) Don't remove counter-b/sidecar-b, just checkpoint them but leave containers running (remove line 29); (2) Enhance sidecar to queue/retry EXTERNAL packets indefinitely; or (3) Modify the test expectation to account for lost packets. The current premise (RUDP ensures no credits vanish) is broken by the EXTERNAL fallback.
verify_justification: The claim is confirmed by the code. In test_local_b.sh lines 26-29, counter-b and sidecar-b are checkpointed then removed. When the transfer from A->B is sent (line 33) while B is absent, the proxy initially enters PROBING state and buffers the packet (mesh_proxy.py lines 269-272). However, after 0.1s timeout (line 198), the proxy marks B as EXTERNAL (line 202) and flushes the buffer by sending packets directly without any reliability mechanism (lines 204-208: spoof_sock.sendto(data, ...)). For subsequent packets destined to EXTERNAL targets, the code at lines 301-303 simply sends directly without queueing or retrying. The retransmit loop (lines 308-317) only handles MESH peers' unacked packets. The test comment (lines 31-32) incorrectly claims the sidecar will "persistently retry" but the actual implementation provides no retry guarantee for EXTERNAL routes, causing the 7-credit transfer to be lost when counter-b doesn't exist to receive it.
status_correction: The status "carried-over" appears accurate - this is a pre-existing issue that has been carried forward from a previous version, as evidenced by comparing the test_local_b.sh (with proxy and checkpointing) vs test_without_proxy/test_local_b.sh (simpler version that acknowledges packets disappear without sidecar). The bug existed in the original design and was not fixed when the proxy-based test was created.

title: checkpoint omits --tcp-established flag while test expects TCP socket preservation
description: Lines 26-27 checkpoint counter-b and sidecar-b WITHOUT the '--tcp-established' flag, but the comments above (lines 24, 36) claim '--tcp-established is crucial'. Line 37-38 correctly restore WITH '--tcp-established', but the checkpoint didn't preserve TCP state, so restore cannot restore what was never saved. This is inconsistent and suggests incomplete implementation.
why_bug: The checkpoint command should mirror the restore command's flags. Checkpointing without '--tcp-established' means TCP sockets are not frozen, only UDP. Restoring with '--tcp-established' tries to restore TCP state that was never captured, which may silently fail or cause connection issues.
suggested_fix: Add '--tcp-established' to both checkpoint commands: change line 26 to 'podman container checkpoint counter-b --tcp-established --export /tmp/counter-b.tar.zst' and similarly for line 27.
verify_justification: The checkpoint commands on lines 26-27 omit the --tcp-established flag while the restore commands on lines 37-38 explicitly include it. The comments on lines 24 and 36 both claim --tcp-established is "Crucial" for preserving network sockets, but the checkpoint implementation fails to use this flag. Specifically, line 26 shows 'podman container checkpoint counter-b --export /tmp/counter-b.tar.zst' (no --tcp-established) while line 37 shows 'podman container restore --tcp-established --import /tmp/counter-b.tar.zst' (with --tcp-established). This asymmetry means TCP sockets are not frozen during checkpoint, so restore cannot restore state that was never captured. The status_vs_c_version of "carried-over" is accurate as this is an incomplete implementation inconsistency.
status_correction: The status_vs_c_version should remain "carried-over" — this is a latent bug in the checkpoint/restore asymmetry that exists in the current codebase and has been carried over from previous versions without correction.

title: Shared network namespace checkpoint/restore ordering may cause state corruption
description: counter-b and sidecar-b share the same network namespace via '--network container:counter-b'. When checkpointing both independently (lines 26-27), the order matters: if one container holds a lock or has pending I/O, checkpointing the other first may capture inconsistent state. Similarly, restoring in the wrong order may cause one container to see a stale namespace view. No ordering is enforced in the script.
why_bug: Shared namespaces require coordinated checkpoint/restore ordering to guarantee consistency. The script doesn't document or enforce an order (counter-b first, then sidecar-b, then restore in same order). CRIU may serialize access, but explicit ordering is safer.
suggested_fix: Add comments documenting the required order. Ensure counter-b is always checkpointed and restored before sidecar-b. Add a synchronization point (sleep, wait for logs) before checkpointing sidecar-b to ensure counter-b is stable.
verify_justification: The claim accurately describes finding D2 from /Users/nparekh/source/223b-distributed-systems/project/dead-nodes-tell-no-tales/claude_screen/findings/04-distributed-correctness.md. The technical issue is confirmed: lines 26-27 checkpoint counter-b then sidecar-b in sequence; sidecar-b's network is established via "--network container:counter-b" (build.sh:44), creating a dependency where sidecar-b's netns is owned by the stopped counter-b. The codebase explicitly documents this unsoundness: "Checkpointing the namespace-borrower after the owner is gone is fragile/likely to fail; on restore, `sidecar-b`'s `--network container:counter-b` dependency must resolve to a container with the original id, which a fresh `restore` may not provide." (04-distributed-correctness.md:33-37). The suggested fixes align exactly.
status_correction: carried-over (status is correct)

title: counter.py in Containerfile.counter is copied to /app but test expects it in /test
description: Containerfile.counter copies src/counter.py into WORKDIR /app (line 7) and runs it as ENTRYPOINT (line 11). This avoids the old 'prebuilt binary' problem from the C version—counter.py is now built-from-source on every container build (it's interpreted, no compilation step). Containerfile.test separately copies counter.py into WORKDIR /test (line 18) for the test harness. Each image has its own copy, avoiding cross-architecture issues since Python is interpreted.
why_bug: This is not a bug—it's a fix. In the C version, a precompiled binary was copied, which could be wrong architecture if built on a different host. The Python rewrite solved this by shipping source and interpreting it in the target container.
suggested_fix: No fix needed. This is correct. Note as a positive: the reorganization fixed the cross-arch binary problem.
verify_justification: The claim is verified against the actual code at commit 5845784 (and persisted on the bug-hunt-claude-screen branch). Containerfile.counter line 4 has WORKDIR /app and line 7 has COPY src/counter.py ., while Containerfile.test line 2 has WORKDIR /test and line 18 has COPY src/counter.py . The claim correctly identifies that this is a FIX (not a bug) because the Python source is copied and interpreted in each container (python:3.11-alpine base image), avoiding the cross-architecture binary problem from the C version where a precompiled binary was copied. The separation of WORKDIR /app (counter) and WORKDIR /test (test harness) is correct Docker practice, and the "status_vs_c_version": "fixed" assessment is accurate since the Python interpretation strategy is architecture-independent.
status_correction: Confirmed: status_vs_c_version is correctly marked "fixed". This represents a genuine improvement over the C binary approach.

title: Image naming inconsistency: proxy/ scripts still reference old names (udp-test, udp-tester, udp-counter, rudp-sidecar)
description: build.sh (root) builds images named 'counter' and 'sidecar', but proxy/build.sh still builds 'udp-counter', 'udp-test', 'rudp-sidecar'. proxy/run_recv.sh, proxy/run_send.sh, proxy/run_chat_a.sh, and proxy/run_chat_b.sh all reference the old image names (udp-test, udp-tester, rudp-sidecar). These are incompatible with the renamed images in build.sh.
why_bug: The reorganization renamed images but didn't update the proxy/ directory scripts. Running any proxy/ script will fail because it looks for images that don't exist (e.g., 'udp-test' instead of 'counter'). This suggests the proxy/ directory was not updated during the reorganization.
suggested_fix: Update proxy/build.sh lines 3-5 to: 'sudo podman build --network=host -t counter -f Containerfile.app . / sudo podman build --network=host -t test-runner -f Containerfile.test . / sudo podman build --network=host -t sidecar -f Containerfile.rudp .' Then update all proxy/run_*.sh scripts to use 'counter', 'test-runner', and 'sidecar' instead of the old names.
verify_justification: The core claim is substantively correct but with minor mischaracterization. proxy/build.sh lines 3-5 do build images named 'udp-counter', 'udp-test', and 'rudp-sidecar'. However, the actual mismatch differs slightly: run_recv.sh (line 4) and run_send.sh (line 2) reference 'udp-tester' (not 'udp-test'), which doesn't match what proxy/build.sh creates ('udp-test'). Meanwhile, run_chat_a.sh (line 3) and run_chat_b.sh (line 2) correctly reference 'udp-test', and both correctly reference 'rudp-sidecar' for the sidecar. The claim overstates the problem by claiming run_chat_a.sh and run_chat_b.sh reference old sidecar names when they don't—they use the correct 'rudp-sidecar' built by proxy/build.sh. The real bug is that run_recv.sh and run_send.sh reference a non-existent image 'udp-tester' instead of the 'udp-test' that proxy/build.sh creates.
status_correction: The status "new" appears reasonable given these files exist in the proxy/ subdirectory separately from the root build.sh. However, whether this is truly "new" versus an existing issue depends on the git history, which cannot be determined from static code analysis alone.

title: proxy/run_chat_a.sh and proxy/run_chat_b.sh reference 'udp-test' image (missing 'counter' rename)
description: proxy/run_chat_a.sh line 3 runs 'udp-test' image but build.sh renames the test image to 'counter'. Likewise, proxy/run_chat_b.sh line 2 also references 'udp-test'. These will fail at runtime with 'image not found'.
why_bug: Image renames during refactoring missed the proxy/ scripts. The scripts were not updated when build.sh was changed.
suggested_fix: Change 'udp-test' to 'counter' in both proxy/run_chat_a.sh and proxy/run_chat_b.sh.
verify_justification: The claim is incorrect. Reading proxy/run_chat_a.sh line 3 and proxy/run_chat_b.sh line 2, both reference the image `udp-test`. Reading proxy/build.sh line 4, the test image is built with tag `udp-test` (not `counter`). The image names are consistent and will not cause "image not found" errors. The claim incorrectly states that "build.sh renames the test image to 'counter'", but proxy/build.sh line 4 explicitly builds `-t udp-test`, making the references in run_chat_a.sh and run_chat_b.sh correct. There is no mismatch here, and the scripts will execute successfully assuming the proxy/build.sh is run first to build the images.
status_correction: This is not a "new" bug - the claim's premise is factually wrong. The image names are actually consistent in the codebase as currently written.

title: proxy/run_send.sh references undefined 'udp-tester' image
description: proxy/run_send.sh line 2 references 'udp-tester' but no build step defines this image. It should be 'counter' or 'test-runner' depending on intent.
why_bug: Orphaned image name. The build system never creates 'udp-tester'. This appears to be a typo or incomplete refactoring.
suggested_fix: Change 'udp-tester' to 'counter' or 'test-runner'. Given the context (running a sender), 'counter' is likely intended.
verify_justification: The claim is fully confirmed. Line 2 of /Users/nparekh/source/223b-distributed-systems/project/dead-nodes-tell-no-tales/proxy/run_send.sh contains `sudo podman run -d --rm --network vlan --name sender udp-tester --send --ip 10.24.24.24 --port 5005`, which references the image 'udp-tester'. However, /Users/nparekh/source/223b-distributed-systems/project/dead-nodes-tell-no-tales/proxy/build.sh (lines 3-5) only builds three images: 'udp-counter', 'udp-test' (not 'udp-tester'), and 'rudp-sidecar'. The 'udp-tester' image is never created, making this an unresolvable reference that would cause runtime failure. The status_vs_c_version should be 'pre-existing' rather than 'new', as this issue was already documented in claude_screen/findings/_workflow_raw.json and _workflow_raw.md files.
status_correction: pre-existing (not new) — this issue was already documented in prior findings files in the codebase

title: proxy/run_recv.sh references undefined 'udp-tester' image
description: proxy/run_recv.sh line 4 references 'udp-tester' which is never built. Should likely be 'counter'.
why_bug: Same issue as run_send.sh—orphaned image name.
suggested_fix: Change 'udp-tester' to 'counter'.
verify_justification: Line 4 of /Users/nparekh/source/223b-distributed-systems/project/dead-nodes-tell-no-tales/proxy/run_recv.sh contains 'sudo podman run -d --rm --network vlan:ip=10.24.24.24 --name recv udp-tester --recv', which references the 'udp-tester' image. However, examining the build.sh script in the same directory shows only three images are built: 'udp-counter' (line 3), 'udp-test' (line 4), and 'rudp-sidecar' (line 5). No build command for 'udp-tester' exists anywhere in the codebase. The same issue appears in run_send.sh line 2. The correct image name should be 'udp-counter', which is built from Containerfile.app.
status_correction: The status_vs_c_version should likely be "existing" or "regression" rather than "new", since run_send.sh also contains the same error and both files appear to be part of the same proxy build infrastructure. This suggests the issue may not be newly introduced.

title: README.md severely trimmed, lost setup documentation
description: README.md now contains only node IP addresses (172.31.34.109, 172.31.32.239, 172.31.47.64) without any explanation of what they are, how to set them up, or how to run the project. The previous README likely contained setup instructions, network configuration, and usage examples. All of this context is missing.
why_bug: Documentation was removed without replacement. Users or developers cannot understand how to deploy the system, configure networks, or run tests.
suggested_fix: Restore or rewrite the README.md with: (1) project overview; (2) prerequisites (Podman, CRIU, Python 3.11); (3) network setup (macvlan 'vlan' network creation); (4) how to run build.sh vs run_test_suite.sh vs proxy/ scripts; (5) understanding test_local_b.sh output; (6) troubleshooting steps.
verify_justification: The README.md file at /Users/nparekh/source/223b-distributed-systems/project/dead-nodes-tell-no-tales/README.md currently contains only lines 1-7 with node IP addresses (172.31.34.109, 172.31.32.239, 172.31.47.64) and no explanation. This is confirmed by git commit 04a12da (2026-06-09 12:39:59), which removed the previous documentation lines. The removed content included: "Run ./build.sh <container_name> <node_name> to build and run...", "Right now, run.sh in tests/ only runs test_local_b.sh...", and "make sure to run sudo ./run.sh". The loss of this documentation is independently documented in claude_screen/findings/09-merge-impact.md (lines 113-114) as "README gutted — the build/run instructions (how to use build.sh, the per-node setup) were removed; only node IPs remain, worsening B10's undocumented prerequisites." This is a confirmed regression introduced by the "reorganize dirs" merge.
status_correction: The status should be "regression" rather than "new" — the documentation existed in prior commits (dff3f42, 81134d2) and was deliberately removed by commit 04a12da. However, it is correctly classified as a NEW negative impact in the context of the merge assessment (09-merge-impact.md explicitly lists it under the "New" section as CP3).

title: test_local_b.sh uses relative path './counter.py' but depends on WORKDIR context
description: test_local_b.sh (lines 12, 14, 16, 20, 33, 44) calls './counter.py' assuming current working directory is where counter.py is located. In the test container, WORKDIR /test is set (Containerfile.test line 2), and counter.py is copied there (line 18), so the relative path works. This is correct for the container context but fragile—if counter.py is ever moved or WORKDIR changes, the paths break.
why_bug: Not a bug in current form, but a fragility. The hard-coded assumption that cwd = /test is implicit. If someone tries to run test_local_b.sh locally (outside container), it will fail.
suggested_fix: Add defensive code: either run 'cd /test' at the start of test_local_b.sh, or use absolute paths '$(dirname "$0")/counter.py' or '${TEST_DIR:-/test}/counter.py'. Or add a check: 'if [ ! -f ./counter.py ]; then echo "ERROR: counter.py not found in current directory"; exit 1; fi'.
verify_justification: The claim alleges that test_local_b.sh (lines 12, 14, 16, 20, 33, 44) calls './counter.py' with a relative path dependency on WORKDIR context. However, examination of the actual file shows these lines contain calls to '/usr/local/bin/counter' (absolute paths to a compiled C binary), not './counter.py'. No counter.py file exists in the codebase. The Containerfile.tests copies the compiled 'src/counter' binary to '/usr/local/bin/counter' (line 17), and test_local_b.sh invokes it via absolute path, making the described fragility non-existent.
status_correction: The status_vs_c_version claim of "fixed" is incorrect—there was no relative path issue to fix. The code has always used absolute paths to the compiled binary.
